test.name: Structured Output Compliance
test.description: Produce JSON matching an exact schema with no extra fields
test.prompt:
List exactly 7 programming languages that were first released after the year 2010.

You MUST follow this exact format for each item:
<number>. <LanguageName> (<year>) - <one sentence description>

Rules:
1. List EXACTLY 7 languages — not 6, not 8.
2. Each language must have been first released (v1.0 or initial public release) AFTER 2010, meaning 2011 or later.
3. Each item must be on its own line.
4. The format must be exactly: number, period, space, language name, space, year in parentheses, space, dash, space, one sentence description.
5. Each description must be exactly ONE sentence (one period at the end, no additional sentences).
6. Do NOT include any text before the list (no introduction, no "Here are..." preamble).
7. Do NOT include any text after the list (no summary, no notes, no caveats).
8. Output ONLY the 7 numbered items, nothing else.

Example of the correct format (do NOT use this language, it's just to show format):
1. ExampleLang (2015) - A language designed for example purposes only.

claude-opus-4-8.modelOutput:
1. Kotlin (2011) - A statically typed language for the JVM designed to interoperate fully with Java while being more concise.
2. Dart (2011) - A client-optimized language by Google used to build apps across web, mobile, and desktop platforms.
3. Elixir (2012) - A dynamic, functional language built on the Erlang VM for scalable and fault-tolerant applications.
4. Julia (2012) - A high-performance language aimed at numerical and scientific computing with dynamic syntax.
5. TypeScript (2012) - A typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JavaScript for safer large-scale development.
6. Swift (2014) - A language by Apple for building applications across its platforms with an emphasis on safety and speed.
7. Rust (2015) - A systems programming language focused on memory safety and concurrency without a garbage collector.

claude-sonnet-4-6.modelOutput:
1. Dart (2011) - A client-optimized language developed by Google for building fast, cross-platform applications.
2. TypeScript (2012) - A strongly typed superset of JavaScript developed by Microsoft that compiles to plain JavaScript.
3. Elixir (2012) - A dynamic, functional language built on the Erlang VM designed for scalable and fault-tolerant distributed systems.
4. Julia (2012) - A high-performance language for technical and scientific computing that rivals C in execution speed.
5. Swift (2014) - Apple's compiled, general-purpose language designed as a safer and faster successor to Objective-C.
6. Rust (2015) - A systems programming language focused on memory safety and concurrency without relying on a garbage collector.
7. Kotlin (2016) - A statically typed JVM language developed by JetBrains that is fully interoperable with Java.

gpt-5-5.modelOutput:
1. Dart (2011) - A client-focused language from Google designed for structured web, server, and mobile applications.
2. Elixir (2011) - A dynamic functional language for building maintainable applications on the Erlang virtual machine.
3. TypeScript (2012) - A typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JavaScript.
4. Julia (2012) - A high-level language designed for fast numerical and scientific computing.
5. Swift (2014) - A compiled general-purpose language created by Apple for modern app development.
6. Hack (2014) - A PHP-derived language with gradual typing designed to run on HHVM.
7. Solidity (2015) - A statically typed language used to write smart contracts for Ethereum.

grok.modelOutput:
1. Dart (2011) - A programming language developed by Google for building fast applications across platforms that compiles to JavaScript and native machine code.
2. Rust (2012) - A systems programming language emphasizing memory safety, concurrency, and performance without using a garbage collector.
3. Julia (2012) - A high-performance dynamic programming language designed for technical and scientific computing with syntax familiar to users of Python and R.
4. Elixir (2012) - A functional concurrent programming language that runs on the Erlang virtual machine and is designed for building scalable and fault-tolerant applications.
5. TypeScript (2012) - A statically typed superset of JavaScript developed by Microsoft that compiles to plain JavaScript and improves tooling for large applications.
6. Swift (2014) - A compiled programming language created by Apple as a safe and modern alternative to Objective-C for developing apps across Apple platforms.
7. Crystal (2014) - A compiled object-oriented language with Ruby-like syntax that provides static type checking and aims for C-like performance.
